perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall protect the disclosed Confidential Informat ion by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information as the Recipient uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall endeavor to protect the disclosed Confidential Information to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Confidential Information, using a degree of care they find appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall protect' to 'shall endeavor to protect' and replacing 'no less than a reasonable degree of care' with 'degree of care they find appropriate' introduces ambiguity and reduces the obligation, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which is undermined by the subjective 'degree of care they find appropriate.' The law requires more than just an 'endeavor' to protect, but requires a duty to keep confidentiality.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the 'shall protect' obligation with 'shall endeavor to protect' and replaces the requirement for 'no less than a reasonable degree of care' with 'degree of care they find appropriate.' According to the law explanation, CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which is undermined by the subjective standard introduced in the changed text. The scraped law snippet identifies the relevant statute, the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) under California law. The original contract mandates a minimum standard of care. The modified contract introduces ambiguity and a lower standard, potentially violating the UTSA's requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which makes the change a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation also agrees that the change reduces obligation, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall (i) 2 NDA Rev. 3 -14-14 Form NDA -3 (Supplier) limit access to all Confidential Information to its employees who shall reasonably require access to the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth above, (ii) shall insure that all such employees have signed a nondisc losure agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement, (iii) shall use the Confidential Information solely in connection with the Purpose, and (iv) shall use due diligence to insure that all such persons comply with the provisions of this Agr eement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall (i) limit access to Confidential Information to employees as they deem necessary, (ii) encourage employees to sign a nondisclosure agreement, (iii) shall primarily use Confidential Information in connection with the Purpose, and (iv) shall attempt to ensure compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall reasonably require' to 'as they deem necessary' weakens the access control. Changing 'shall insure that all such employees have signed' to 'encourage employees to sign' makes NDA signing optional. Changing 'solely' to 'primarily' allows other uses. Changing 'use due diligence to insure' to 'attempt to ensure' lowers the standard of compliance enforcement. All of these weaken the trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which includes limiting access, ensuring NDAs are in place, and restricting use. These changes make these requirements discretionary, undermining the reasonable efforts standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changes in the contract weaken the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the requirement to maintain secrecy. Specifically, changing 'shall reasonably require' to 'as they deem necessary' makes access control less strict. 'Shall insure that all such employees have signed' becoming 'encourage employees to sign' weakens the NDA requirement. Similarly, 'solely' changing to 'primarily' and 'use due diligence to insure' becoming 'attempt to ensure' lowers the standards of compliance. The Law Explanation states CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, including limiting access, ensuring NDAs, and restricting use. The altered contract language undermines this 'reasonable efforts' standard by introducing ambiguity and discretion where there was previously obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the relevant section of California Civil Code addressing the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Though this snippet alone does not provide specifics of 'reasonable effort', in conjunction with the Law Explanation, it underscores the relevance of CUTSA in assessing the contract's terms.

perturbation.original_text: Within ten (10) days after any written request by Sanmina, the Recipient shall promptly return all copies of the Confidential Information except copies required for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity and in such case the obligations hereunder shall survive until such copies are destroyed .
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written request by Sanmina, the Recipient should endeavor to return copies of the Confidential Information, except for copies the Recipient believes are needed for business purposes, at which point the obligations hereunder may continue until such copies are addressed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly return' to 'should endeavor to return' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'required for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity' with 'Recipient believes are needed for business purposes' broadens the exception. Changing 'shall survive until such copies are destroyed' to 'may continue until such copies are addressed' makes the duration of confidentiality ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and this modified clause undermines that requirement by making the return of confidential information discretionary and extending the permitted exceptions.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return confidential information, broadens exceptions for retaining copies, and makes the duration of confidentiality ambiguous. The original text states 'shall promptly return,' which is a clear obligation. The changed text says 'should endeavor to return,' weakening the obligation. The original text limits exceptions to 'required for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity.' The changed text broadens this to 'Recipient believes are needed for business purposes.' The Law Explanation states that CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and the modified clause undermines that requirement. While the provided snippet does not explicitly state the requirements of CUTSA, the Law Explanation directly ties the changes to a potential violation of CUTSA by undermining the requirement to maintain secrecy. This, coupled with the Perturbation Explanation, indicates the changed text creates a contradiction, because it introduces ambiguity and weakens legal obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that the state courts of Santa Clara County, California and the federal courts located in the Northern District of the State of California shall have exclusive jurisdiction and venue to adjudicate any and all disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that the state courts of Santa Clara County, California, and the federal courts located in the Northern District of the State of California may have jurisdiction and venue to adjudicate disputes related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall have exclusive jurisdiction' to 'may have jurisdiction' removes the certainty of where disputes will be resolved. It introduces ambiguity and allows for arguments that other courts might also have jurisdiction, increasing the cost and complexity of litigation.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 USC 1391 outlines rules for venue in federal courts, agreements specifying venue are generally upheld. Changing the clause to 'may have jurisdiction' creates ambiguity that could lead a court to disregard the parties' intended venue, undermining the predictability and enforceability of the agreement regarding venue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall have exclusive jurisdiction' to 'may have jurisdiction' introduces ambiguity regarding where disputes will be resolved. According to the Law Explanation, agreements specifying venue are generally upheld, and this change undermines the predictability and enforceability of the venue agreement. The modification weakens the original obligation, creating uncertainty and potentially leading to disputes over jurisdiction. This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation, which notes that such ambiguity can increase litigation costs and complexity. The provided law snippets (28 U.S. Code § 1391) outline the general rules for venue in federal courts, and while they don't explicitly prohibit parties from agreeing to a specific venue, the change makes it less certain whether the parties' intended venue will be respected.
